sections.label: P
sections.title: P is defined by its parametric equation

spans.text: Define a fat line as the region between two parallel lines. The curve intersection algorithm described here begins by computing a fat line which bounds one of the two Bezier curves. Similar bounds have been suggested in References 5, 6 and 7. Denote by I the line Po-Pr. A fat line is chosen parallel to L, as shown in Figure 1. If I is defined in its normalized implicit equation a x+b y+c=0 \quad\left(a^{2}+b^{2}=1\right)

After three Bézier clips on each curve, the intersection is computed to within six digits of accuracy (see Table
Clipping to other fat lines

The fat line defined above provides a nearly optimal Bézier clip in many cases. However, it is clear that any pair of parallel lines which bound the curve can serve as a fat line. In many cases, a fat line perpendicular to the line
provides a larger Bézier clip than does the fat line parallel to the line
. Figure 8 shows

such a case. It is suggested that in general it works best to examine both fat lines to determine which one provides the largest clip. This extra overhead results in a slightly lower average execution time.

curve by at least 20%, subdivide the 'longest' curve (largest remaining parameter interval) and intersect the shorter curve, respectively, with the two halves of the longer curve. This heuristic, applied recursively if needed, allows computation of arbitrary numbers of intersections.
Rational curves
If
is a rational Bézier curve
with control point coordinates
and corresponding non-negative weights
, the Bézier clip computation is modified as follows. Substituting equation (20) into equation (17) and clearing the denominator yields:
However, unlike the nonrational case, the intersection of
with a fat line cannot be represented as
. Instead,
must be clipped independently against each of the two lines bounding the fat line. Thus, ranges of
are identified for which
These ranges are identified using the Bézier clipping technique as previously outlined.
The focus construction proceeds as follows. If
defines any vector function (not necessarily of unit length) which is perpendicular to
for all values of
, then the curve
(where
is any function of
) can serve as a focus curve. is itself a Bézier curve of degree
, whose control points
are

(see Figure 16). This discussion deals only with hodographs and foci of polynomial curves. Hodographs of rational Bézier curves are discussed in Reference 12, following which the construction of a focus parallels the present discussion. Graphically, the tangent vector
is expressed as a vector from the hodograph origin to the point
on the hodograph (see Figure 17). By rotating the hodograph through
about its origin, a vector function
is obtained which defines vectors perpendicular to
(see Figure 18). In creating the focus
is chosen to be a degree-one polynomial
which satisfies the condition
. This is a heuristic decision, motivated by the observation that if
begins and ends at the same point, it will tend to cover a relatively small area. The examples in Figures 13-15 illustrate that this choice generally works well. The coefficients
and
are solved from the linear equation

Thus, even curves with inflection points (as in Figure 14) can have a reasonable focus. This focus construction behaves well when the curve is subdivided into smaller segments, quickly converging to the centre of curvature of their respective curve segments. Thus, even though a curve may initially have an impracticably large focus, the size of the focus will shrink quickly as the curve is subdivided. Figures 19 and 20 show the curve in Figure 15 after it has been split into two and four segments. The resulting foci appear nearly optimally small.
Other focus heuristics

After some experimentation, the authors are confident that the focus heuristic is competitive. A few other choices present themselves. first, one could simply compute a line segment through which all lines normal to a given curve pass. This has the virtue that the focus
is a simpler entity than our degree
focus, but more computation must go into computing the focus. A second choice is to use the evolute
of the given curve as its focus. The evolute is the locus of the centres
can be expressed as a tensor product polynomial in Bernstein form in
and
The top view of the patch
corresponding to Figure 23 is shown in Figure 24. A side view of the
patch, looking down the
axis, is shown in Figure 25. In this side view, portions of
which are completely above or beneath the
-axis correspond to portions of
for which no normal line intersects any point on

patch. Therefore, it is certain that regions of the
-axis which lie completely outside the convex hull of the projected
control points represent portions of
for which no normal line intersects any point on

and

Collinear normal algorithm

All the tools have now been gathered to create an algorithm for computing all lines which are simultaneously perpendicular to two curves. Continuing the example in the previous subsection, we clip away portions of
which do not have normals through
. Those portions are shown in fine pen width in Figure 26. Next, compute a focus for the remaining segment of
, as shown in Figure 26, and clip away regions of
whose normal lines do not pass through
s focus. The remaining portion of
is shown in heavy pen width in Figure 27, and also its focus

. This iteration continues as recorded in Table 2. After four iterations, the point of tangency is actually determined to eight digits of accuracy. If an iteration fails to reduce the parameter range of either curve by at least, say, 20%, there may be more than one collinear normal. The remedy is to split one if the curves in half and to compute the collinea ormals of each half with the other curve, using a stac data structure to store pairs of curve segments.
TIMING COMPARISONS
The Bézier trim curve intersection algorithm has been implemented and some timing comparisons have been

Our experience is that, for curves of degree less than five, the implicitization algorithm is typically between one and three times faster than the Bézier clip algorithm, which in turn is typically between two and ten times faster than the other two algorithms. For curves of degree higher than four, the Bézier clipping algorithm generally wins. The algorithm for computing tangent intersections can typically compute a tangent intersection faster than the Bézier clip curve intersector can compute two well-spaced simple intersections. The algorithm for computing tangent intersections is designed for firstorder tangencies, and its performance degrades for higher-order tangencies.

math.display_latex: d_{i}=\sum_{\substack{i+k=i \\ j \in\{0, \ldots, n\} \\ k \in\{0, \ldots, n-1\}}} \frac{\binom{n}{j}\binom{n-1}{k}}{\binom{2 n-1}{i}} n\left(\mathbf{P}_{k+1}-\mathbf{P}_{k}\right) \cdot\left(\mathbf{P}_{j}-\mathbf{F}\right)

compiled_targets.mathml: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>d</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>i</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>i</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>j</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo stretchy="false">{</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>…</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo stretchy="false">}</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>k</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo stretchy="false">{</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>…</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn><mo stretchy="false">}</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></msub><mfrac><mrow><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo></mrow><mrow><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo></mrow></mfrac><mi>n</mi><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>−</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msub><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow><mi>·</mi><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>−</mo><mi>𝐅</mi><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow></mrow></math>

math.display_latex: d_{i j}=\sum_{\substack{k,+l=i \\ 1 \in\{0, \ldots, n\} \\ k \in\{0, \ldots, n-1\}}} \frac{\binom{n}{j}\binom{n-1}{k}}{\binom{2 n-1}{j}} n\left(\mathbf{P}_{k+1}-\mathbf{P}_{k}\right) \cdot\left(\mathbf{P}_{l}-\mathbf{F}_{j}\right)

compiled_targets.mathml: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" display="inline"><mrow><msub><mi>d</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mstyle scriptlevel="1"><mtable><mtr><mtd><mi>k</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>+</mo><mi>l</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>i</mi></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mn>1</mn><mo>∈</mo><mo stretchy="false">{</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>…</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo stretchy="false">}</mo></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>k</mi><mo>∈</mo><mo stretchy="false">{</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>…</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn><mo stretchy="false">}</mo></mtd></mtr></mtable></mstyle></mrow></msub><mfrac><mrow><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mi>n</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo></mrow><mrow><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">(</mo><mfrac linethickness="0"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>n</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo minsize="2.047em" maxsize="2.047em">)</mo></mrow></mfrac><mi>n</mi><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>k</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>−</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow></msub><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow><mi>·</mi><mrow><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="prefix">(</mo><msub><mi>𝐏</mi><mrow><mi>l</mi></mrow></msub><mo>−</mo><msub><mi>𝐅</mi><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow></msub><mo stretchy="true" fence="true" form="postfix">)</mo></mrow></mrow></math>

\(u_{i j}=j / m\). A point on such a patch has coordinates
